text: good morning this is a case of laparoscopic myomectomy this patient has eighteen centimeter intramural myoma in the fundal and the posterior wall of the uterus and we will do laparoscopic myomectomy the four ports is used the first port approximately ten centimeter above the umbilicus
The surgeon positions laparoscopic ports at Palmer's point, the left lumbar region, and a contralateral port on the right side for access during the myomectomy procedure.

The surgeon injects approximately 200 ml of Vasopressin diluted with saline into the uterine tissue using an aspiration needle and XXC syringe, facilitating transient ischemia and hydro dissection to achieve a clear surgical plane.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to incise the muscle and serous layers of the uterus, which has a marble white appearance following the vasopressin injection.

The surgeon enlarges the incision on the uterus, ensuring a good plane is achieved for suction irrigation and dissection. A myoma screw is used to hold the myoma in place during the laparoscopic myomectomy.
The surgeon prepares to introduce the myoma screw through the supraumbilical port to apply traction on the large intramural myoma, requiring a ten-centimeter incision for removal due to its size.

The surgeon enlarges the incision further to facilitate nucleation of the myoma. Suction irrigation is used as the preferred instrument for this step, avoiding the risk of breakage associated with double-action jaw instruments like harmonic or Maryland graspers during aggressive dissection.

The surgeon uses a single jaw traumatic grasper on the left side and a suction irrigation device on the right hand to enucleate the myoma. Blunt dissection is performed to achieve a good plane, separating the muscle layer from the myoma.
The surgeon carefully performs dry dissection to avoid opening the uterine cavity, ensuring the endometrium remains intact. The myoma is gradually being extracted from the uterus.
The surgeon frequently relocates the myoma screw to maintain effective traction during dissection. Depending on the situation, two or three myoma screws may be used to assist in the procedure.

The surgeon extracts a soft myoma from the uterine cavity, noting its texture as it emerges. The softness of the myoma poses challenges, as it may break apart, complicating the establishment of a clear dissection plane.

This type of softness of the myoma is generally encountered once the patient has GnRH. like Luperon, Sinaladil or Busaraline before the surgery. So, previously this patient was given these medications because it was a huge myoma. In spite of giving all the GnRH, the size is approximately eighteen centimeters.

The surgeon completes the slow and careful dissection to fully extract the myoma from the uterine muscle layer. The procedure involves addressing the challenge of closing the site after removing a deep intramural myoma.
The surgeon begins the repair of the uterus by suturing multiple layers to prevent uterine rupture in future pregnancies.

The surgeon begins the uterine repair by applying three layers of suturing. The inner layer is sutured using a Dundee jamming knot in a continuous fashion, followed by an extracorporeal square knot, and then another layer of continuous suturing.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect the remaining muscle attachments, successfully separating the entire myoma from the uterine tissue. The myoma is now completely removed from the surgical field.

The surgeon begins suturing the inner muscle layer using a Dundee jamming knot, which serves as a starter knot. The knot is self-locking, providing secure anchorage for the subsequent continuous suturing of the deep muscle layer.
The surgeon performs continuous suturing in the deep muscle layer of the myoma, ensuring each stitch is locked to maintain tension and prevent loosening.

Bi-directional barb suture like V-lock is also used for this type of repair but in my opinion, for the continuous suturing with the use of good knotting skill with the vehicle is much better. V-lock is not that much secure because it cannot withstand that much tension in between the layers. So that's why

The surgeon performs interlocking continuous suturing on the deeper muscle layer, followed by interrupted extracorporeal square knots to reduce the gap. A final continuous suturing layer is applied to close the serous layer, completing the three-layer closure technique.

The surgeon performs continuous suturing inside the open cavity, using two layers of sutures to approximate the tissue. Due to the large incision size, the sutures are applied in segments, with each segment covering five to seven centimeters, as the suture length is limited to twenty centimeters in laparoscopic procedures.

The surgeon completes the final locking of the continuous suture, keeping the needle active for traction. A new continuous suturing is initiated from below using a dandy jamming knot, where the suture is passed through an existing loop to secure the knot automatically.

The surgeon performs continuous suturing from below upwards on the inner muscle layer of the uterus during a myomectomy, using a dandy jamming knot and Aberdeen termination for the innermost layer of a three-layer repair.

The surgeon applies a surgeon's knot on both ends of the needle to terminate the suture, using a double wrap technique. The inner muscle layer is visibly approximated and closed, completing the suturing from above down.
The surgeon uses No.-one Vicryl suture to reinforce the muscle layer, aiming to decrease bleeding and prevent weakness or gaps in the tissue.
The surgeon applies an extracorporeal Mishra's knot as a tension suture to approximate the edges of the surgical site, visibly reducing the large gap left by the removal of the 18-centimeter myoma.

The surgeon removes the needle and cuts it, then applies a series of knots using a darker knot pusher. The configuration includes one hitch, three winds, and three locks, converting the slip knot into a locked knot to prevent slipping.

The surgeon applies multiple extracorporeal knots with deep bites to obliterate dead space and reinforce the edges of the surgical site. The tension sutures visibly approximate the edges, minimizing the risk of seroma formation in the cavity.
The surgeon applies a blanket suture using an extracorporeal knot, followed by a mattress suture to approximate the tissue edges. The sutures are locked multiple times to ensure secure closure.
The surgeon demonstrates the use of Mishra's knot for closing the tension suture in the myoma, utilizing a Clark knot pusher to slide and lock the knot effectively.

The surgeon uses an endoske needle with a 30 mm round body needle to take a deep bite, approximating the edges of the surgical site. The extracorporeal knot technique effectively closes the gap left by the removal of the myoma, minimizing tension and preventing bleeding.
The surgeon observes the decreasing gap between tissue edges as each extracorporeal knot is slid and locked to prevent slipping back, ensuring stable approximation of the edges.

The surgeon performs continuous suturing to approximate the serosal edges, using a Dandy jamming knot for self-locking. The bleeding has stopped, and the edges are tightly approximated with interlocking sutures, ensuring a secure closure.
The surgeon hands the suture to the assistant, who maintains traction while the surgeon takes a bite within the loop, ensuring the suture self-locks.
The surgeon applies a continuous blanket suture to tightly approximate the serous layer during the myomectomy procedure. The assistant holds the suture as the surgeon continues the stitching process.

The surgeon completes the termination of discontinuous suturing using a surgeon's knot, utilizing the tail of a previous suture. The uterus is visibly round with no bleeding present. The procedure transitions to morcellation of the myoma using a Gynecare morcellator, preparing to fragment the large myoma laparoscopically.

The surgeon performs morcellation of the myoma using a Gynecare morcellator. The tissue is carefully brought towards the morcellator for safe morcellation, ensuring the instrument's surface remains visible to maintain control and prevent errors.
